system_prompt:
You are writing the "statement of outputs" — a human-readable narrative that
synthesizes everything the book teaches into a coherent description of the master/work's method.

The user will give you the book-level distillation, the chapter summaries, and
the structured systems-draft JSON you just derived. Write the statement.

Structure:

  # <Work Title> — Statement of Outputs

  ## Overview
  (2-3 paragraphs on what the book is and what it teaches, citing the
  book-level distillation.)

  ## Systems
  For each system in the systems-draft, one ## section. For each:
    - one paragraph describing the system in plain language
    - the system's members listed
    - the traversal rules and modification rules listed with their
      engine_payload kinds shown in code formatting
    - citations back to the relevant chapter summaries that backed each
      claim (e.g. "(Chapter 2 summary; ch02.md quotes)")

  ## Pending Work
  List every `_pending:<kebab>` engine_payload.kind value with what it
  signals. If there are none, omit this section.

  ## Provenance Notes
  Brief: what's drawn from what. Note any chapters that didn't yield a
  system and why.

This is the human-facing artifact. Be precise, cite, and don't invent
content outside the summaries and quote files.

# Book-level distillation — vol-1-how-to-play-jazz-and-improvise

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## The Dual-Brain Framework: Left-Brain Knowledge, Right-Brain Spontaneity

This book teaches jazz improvisation as the product of two complementary mental modes held in dynamic balance. Chapter 1 names this the "right brain–left brain" approach: the left brain masters the finite, learnable content of scales, chords, arpeggios, and chord-scale equivalence; the right brain deploys that content spontaneously, listening intently and responding to the moment. Every subsequent chapter is organized around closing the gap between these two faculties—drilling patterns until they become automatic (left brain), then releasing control so that musical impulse, not calculation, drives phrase-making (right brain). The five prerequisites established in Chapter 1—desire, listening, a practice method, a rhythm section, and self-esteem/discipline—frame the entire volume: technique is necessary but never sufficient; the ear and the creative will are co-equal requirements.

## The Core Vocabulary: Chord Tones, Scales, and the Horizontal/Vertical Distinction

The book's entire technical architecture rests on a small set of terms introduced in Chapters 2 and 3 and reinforced throughout. Chord tones—the 1st, 3rd, 5th, 7th, and 9th of any scale—are the harmonic skeleton of improvisation. Scales are presented horizontally (melodic, linear) while chords appear vertically (simultaneous); this horizontal/vertical metaphor governs how the student thinks about the relationship between melody and harmony at every level. Dorian mode is designated the universal minor scale, preferred over pure minor throughout. The reduced chord-symbol notation introduced in Chapter 15 (C, C7, C-, C∅, C-Δ, C7+9, C7b9) encodes chord-scale equivalence directly: each symbol names not just a chord but the series of usable tones available to the improviser over that chord. The Scale Syllabus that follows organizes scale choices within each chord category by dissonance level, from consonant options at the top to increasingly tense ones below—placing the control of tension and release directly in the improviser's hands.

## The Practice Method: Seven Steps, Twelve Keys, Ear First

The book prescribes an explicit, sequential practice methodology that recurs across chapters. Chapter 2 introduces a seven-step scale practice sequence progressing from root-to-ninth motion through intervals, triads, and seventh chords. Chapter 7 extends this into a 12-step memorization procedure moving from single root notes through scales, triads, seventh and ninth chords, and intervallic patterns like broken thirds, culminating in auditory anticipation—the state where the mind hears the progression before the fingers move. Chapter 18 refines the approach further, prescribing ten canonical warm-up exercises practiced across all keys using six distinct root sequences. The governing rule throughout is ear-first internalization: sing the scale, then play what you sang. The voice is treated as the foundational instrument, and the goal in every chapter is to transfer aurally internalized material to the instrument—never the reverse.

Transposition through all 12 keys is treated not as an advanced skill but as a baseline daily discipline, introduced in Chapter 3 through chromatic transposition of patterns and codified in Chapters 19 and 20 as the organizing principle of all practice. The Circle of Fourths is named in Chapter 19 as the harmonic foundation found everywhere in jazz, and the II–V7 pattern is the primary vehicle for 12-key memorization. The book explicitly targets the problem of students who practice only in comfortable keys: Chapter 14 mandates developing articulation control across the entire range and all keys, not just easy ones.

## Guide Tones, Bebop, and the Architecture of Harmonic Targeting

Chapters 8 and 11 formalize the book's central rule for harmonic navigation: beats 1 and 3 should land on chord tones, with the 3rd and 7th (called Guide-Tones or Goal-Notes) identified as structurally primary. These two degrees determine chord quality and harmonic motion; dominant 7th chords resolve up a perfect 4th, with altered tones moving by half-step to their targets. The bebop scale—introduced in Chapter 8—operationalizes this rule mechanically: by adding a single chromatic passing tone to the dominant 7th, major, minor, or half-diminished scale, the bebop scale creates an 8-tone pattern that automatically aligns chord tones to strong beats in 4/4 time. The added chromatic tone must fall on the upbeat. A key substitution follows from this: the dominant 7th bebop scale functions interchangeably with the minor ii chord it resolves to, allowing a single scale to cover both chords in a ii–V progression. Chapter 16 extends the dominant 7th palette to seven scale options (DOM.7th, Bebop, Lydian Dominant, Whole-tone, Diminished, Diminished Whole-tone, and Spanish/Jewish), each with specific sonic character and deployment criteria.

## Tension and Release: The Governing Aesthetic Principle

The book is organized around a single aesthetic logic: music is made of tension and release, and the improviser's communicative power depends on conscious control of this balance. Chapter 5 introduces tension/release as a melodic architecture concept; Chapter 7 maps specific devices to each pole (fast notes and ascending lines build tension; rests, descending lines, and chord tones release it); Chapter 13 formalizes the four-stage compositional sequence—statement, development, climax, release—and provides paired reference lists of tension-producing and release-producing elements. Chapter 15 organizes the entire Scale Syllabus by dissonance level within each chord category, so the student's choice of scale is itself a choice about where on the tension-release spectrum to operate. "Pretty notes" or color tones—the 6, 7, 9, and ♯4 defined in Chapter 7 for major, dominant, and minor harmonies—are the specific expressive tools for shaping this arc within a phrase.

Rhythmic placement participates in the same logic. Chapter 13 introduces a three-placement system—ahead of the beat, on the beat, behind the beat—each producing a distinct expressive character. Beginners are anchored to the beat before developing personal timing feel. The swing/even-eighths distinction (Chapter 4) is a rhythmic prerequisite: swung eighths for jazz, even eighths for bossa nova and rock, with authentic swing requiring a loose, unburdened feel. Silence is consistently treated as a structural element, not an absence—counted as part of phrase architecture and used to orient listeners within a solo.

## Extended Vocabulary: Blues, Pentatonics, and Chromaticism

The book introduces three layers of color beyond diatonic scale playing, each with explicit rules of deployment. The blues scale (root, ♭3, 4, ♯4, 5, ♭7) is characterized by intentional clash with the underlying harmony—that friction is the blues sound, not an error. It works over static minor chords of two or more measures and is alternated with Dorian for variety; jazz players are instructed to extract licks from it rather than run it as a scalar pattern, and overuse is explicitly warned against. Pentatonic scales (Chapter 9), in both major and minor forms, are similarly positioned as tools for variety rather than primary vocabulary, deployed sparingly; the 4th scale degree is typically avoided in pentatonic construction. Chromaticism (Chapter 10) is framed as the complete musical alphabet—every chromatic approach (from a half step below, from enclosure combining a scale tone above with a chromatic tone below, or descending from a whole step above) targets a chord tone. These three layers—blues, pentatonic, chromatic—are additive colorants applied over the diatonic-and-bebop foundation, not replacements for it.

## Articulation, Audiation, and the Mind–Voice–Instrument Hierarchy

Chapter 14 establishes articulation as the second most important element in jazz playing after note choice. The foundational error is over-tonguing—the "tat tat tat" choppy feel that contradicts modern jazz's legato aesthetic. The book prescribes a progression from heavy to medium to light accents, and names nine articulation types (heavy, medium, light, staccato, legato, breath, throat, stab, jab), with staccato explicitly reserved for advanced players. All articulation exercises must become automatic—practiced with metronome discipline in all keys—before they can sound natural in improvisation.

Undergirding every technical prescription is the mind–voice–instrument hierarchy: musical intention originates in the mind (audiation—Chapter 5 names it directly as hearing before playing), is tested through the voice (the ear-voice-instrument learning sequence of Chapter 15), and only then executes through the instrument. This is the book's deepest methodological commitment. Chapter 7 mandates that rhythm be mentally heard before execution; Chapter 11 insists on ear training before technical completeness; Chapter 17 places listening to recordings at the center of jazz musicianship and states that the ear, not notation, is the best teacher. The book's final prescription in Chapter 20—"play what your mind hears"—is not a coda but a distillation of everything that preceded it: technique is in service of imagination, and imagination is developed through lifelong listening, singing, memorization, and the permission to begin now rather than wait for perfection.

# Chapter 1 — Right Brain - Left Brain & Introduction

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

The most successful musicians balance technical left-brain knowledge (fingerings, scales, chords, and chord-scale equivalence) with creative right-brain spontaneity, a philosophy that underpins this method's signature dual-brain approach to practice. Chapter 1 establishes the foundational technical requirements—scales, chords, and arpeggios—while prescribing a specific practice method: drilling patterns until they become automatic, then improvising spontaneously and creatively over chord progressions while listening intently and analyzing what you hear. The book introduces five prerequisites for jazz improvisation (desire, listening, a practice method, a rhythm section, and self-esteem/discipline) and frames all subsequent content around the elemental building blocks of music—scales, chords, sounds, and silences—which appear in every transcribed jazz solo through common devices like diatonic patterns, chromatic passages, leaps, and rests. Technical exercises serve the functional purpose of developing facility: enabling fingers, tongue, and mind to respond quickly and sharply to musical impulses, bridging the gap between mental conception and physical execution on the instrument.

# Chapter 2 — How To Use & Practice Guide

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 establishes the pedagogical foundation for improvisation-first practice: the book prioritizes playing over exercises, requiring learners to internalize four-bar phrasing, maintain form awareness through beat counting, and memorize key signatures to focus on musicality rather than notation. The master's characteristic moves include concrete recovery techniques—hearing changes by ear using drum accents, moving wrong notes a half-step to find correct ones, and building facility through a seven-step scale practice method that progresses from root-to-ninth motion through intervals to triads and seventh chords. Load-bearing terminology introduced here includes chord tones (the 1st, 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th of any scale), the horizontal/vertical metaphor for scales and chords, Dorian mode as the universal minor scale, and the notation conventions (dash for minor, triangle for major, 7 for dominant seventh) that decode the entire volume.

# Chapter 3 — How To Begin Playing With The Recording

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches a systematic approach to blues improvisation built on progressive mastery: starting with scale practice organized by chord symbol, advancing through pattern isolation and chromatic transposition, and culminating in fluent phrase playing across chord changes. The master's characteristic moves emphasize fingering fluency as the direct pathway to connected improvisation, with specific techniques like legato articulation (particularly for brass), scale memorization by sound, and recognition of common tones between scales to ensure smooth transitions. Key load-bearing terminology introduced includes chorus (one complete pass through the chord/scale progression), chord tones (root, 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th, 11th, 13th), triad (the three-note 1-3-5 subset), and the foundational distinction between chords appearing vertically and scales horizontally—all organized through concrete exercises: whole-note scales, quarter-note scales, five-note subsets, scales in thirds extending to the 9th, and arpeggio drills using triad tones.

# Chapter 4 — Eighth-Note Exercises And Swing

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 introduces extended chords (seventh and ninth chords) as expansions of the triad using the scale's 7th and 9th tones, establishing a tonal hierarchy where roots and thirds form the "family" while 7ths and 9ths add excitement. The chapter's core technical contribution is the swing eighth-note rule—the fundamental distinction between swung eighths for jazz and even eighths for bossa nova and rock styles—each requiring a different rhythmic feel to authentically represent its genre. Key terminology includes seventh chords, ninth chords, the 9th/2nd enharmonic equivalence, and the performance concepts of swing feel and even eighths. The chapter grounds students in structural awareness by emphasizing that jazz phrasing typically organizes in 2, 4, and 8 measure phrases, and reinforces execution details like keeping wind-player fingers close to keys for smooth legato, ultimately teaching that authentic swing demands a loose, unburdened feel without dragging.

# Chapter 5 — Beginning To Improvise & Check List

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 teaches the foundational transition from scale practice to improvisation through a structured pedagogical sequence: beginning with whole-note scales and building toward ninth-chord arpeggios, then moving to actual improvisation constrained to scale tones with free rhythm. The master's characteristic voice emerges in his reframing of "wrong notes" as contextual choices rather than errors, and in his prescriptive rules for expanding beyond common beginner limitations—ascending-only motion, register comfort zones, and monolithic articulation. Load-bearing terminology includes the prearranged-rhythm scaffolding (with explicit exit criteria), audiation (mental hearing before playing), and melodic architecture concepts like tension/release and silence as structural tools. Throughout, the chapter emphasizes that memorized scales free the mind for melodic development, while dynamic contrast, articulation variety, and repetition serve as listener orientation devices in solo construction.

# Chapter 6 — Range, Creativity, and Phrase Construction

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

This chapter teaches the harmonic and technical foundations for expanding improvisational range and expression, centering on the rule that chord tones (1, 3, 5, 7) must anchor strong beats to ensure melodies flow naturally. The master's characteristic pedagogical moves are evident in the prescribed four-step pre-improvisation checklist (listening, scale-chord review, memorization by sharps/flats/fingerings, and learning the form), the methodological progression from one-octave to two-octave scales adapted to student ability, and the mind–voice–instrument hierarchy that justifies technical practice as a means of closing the gap between creative intention and instrumental execution. The load-bearing terminology introduced includes "the form" (the memorized order of scales in a progression), "chord tones" as harmonic anchors, and "range extension," with the unifying principle that technical exercises exist solely to free the instrumentalist to improvise spontaneously rather than to constrain imagination to what the hands already know.

# Chapter 7 — Music Fundamentals For Improvising

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chord tones and strong beats form the foundation for beginning improvised phrases, while rhythm must be mentally heard before execution—a prerequisite for coherence. The chapter maps rhythmic density to emotional effect: fast notes build tension while rests and longer durations create relaxation, with specific "pretty notes" (color tones like the 6, 7, 9, and #4) defined for major, dominant, and minor harmonies to shape this tension-release arc. The centerpiece is a systematic 12-step memorization procedure progressing from single root notes through scales, triads, seventh and ninth chords, and intervallic patterns like broken thirds—designed to culminate in auditory anticipation where the mind hears the progression in advance and fingers find notes automatically. The chapter establishes the method's core philosophy: master the rules thoroughly before breaking them, and develop ear through specific exercises like "drop the needle" listening on play-along recordings.

# Chapter 8 — The Bebop Scale & Ear Training

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Beats 1 and 3 should land on chord tones—roots, thirds, fifths, sevenths, and ninths—so listeners hear the intended harmony and anticipate the melodic direction, a foundational rule formalized as targeting "Guide-Tones" or "Goal-Notes" (with thirds and sevenths being structurally primary). The bebop scale adds a single chromatic tone to the four most-used scales, creating an 8-tone pattern that naturally aligns chord tones to strong beats in 4/4 time; the added tone must always fall on the upbeat to preserve jazz language, and this principle extends across dominant 7th, major, minor, and half-diminished chord qualities. A key substitution technique treats the dominant 7th bebop scale as interchangeable with the minor ii chord it resolves to, allowing a single scale to cover both chords in a ii–V progression. The bebop scale serves as the connective tissue of jazz vocabulary, making it essential for developing the spatial fluency of chord-tone awareness across the fingerboard.

# Chapter 9 — Pentatonic Scale And Its Use

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Pentatonic scales, built from five tones in their major and minor forms, serve as versatile harmonic tools applicable across major, minor, and dominant seventh chords, yet should be deployed sparingly to add variety rather than dominate the musical texture. The minor pentatonic and blues scale share close similarity, differentiated by the blues scale's additional sixth tone, while within any standard scale—particularly the major and dorian modes—multiple pentatonic possibilities exist, with the caveat that the major scale's fourth degree is typically avoided in pentatonic construction. Melodically, the pentatonic frame invites personalization; the player is encouraged to sing through their instrument, allowing phrasing to breathe and flow naturally while making the melody distinctly their own.

# Chapter 10 — Chromaticism

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 teaches chromaticism—the use of half-step intervals to approach and embellish chord tones—as a foundational technique for jazz improvisation across all chord types. The chapter introduces specific chromatic approaches: targeting each chord tone (root, 3rd, 5th, 7th) from a half step below, employing enclosure by combining scale tones above with chromatic approach notes below, and descending through half steps from a whole step above. The master's characteristic move is treating the chromatic scale as a complete musical vocabulary—the "musical alphabet"—that can animate any chord or scale context, reinforcing the earlier lesson that multiple pentatonic subsets exist within parent scales and extending that principle to continuous chromatic movement. Load-bearing terminology includes chromaticism itself, enclosure, approach tones, and the reframing of chromatic resources as tools applicable to major, minor, dominant 7th, and any other chord type.

# Chapter 11 — Playing The Blues

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch11.md`._

Chapter 11 teaches the foundational techniques of blues improvisation, beginning with singing as a direct pathway to authentic musical voice, then progressing through structured methods like practicing on 2–3 notes to internalize rhythmic feel, and mastering the 12-bar blues harmonic structure (basic three-chord progressions and Charlie Parker's descending "Bird Blues" variation). The chapter emphasizes guide tones—particularly the 3rd and 7th of each chord, approached by half-step—as the primary tool for tracking harmonic movement, while the blues scale itself is presented as intentionally clashing with the underlying harmony to create the characteristic blues sound. Across these techniques, the master's characteristic moves include the insistence on ear training before technical completeness (learning roots, then partial scales, then full scales in stages), the practice of mental rehearsal away from the instrument, and the transcription-and-imitation method rooted in jazz tradition, all converging on a single load-bearing principle: leading tones and guide-tone improvisation rule blues playing.

# Chapter 12 — Seventh Chords & Time And Feeling

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch12.md`._

The blues scale (root, ♭3, 4, ♯4, 5, ♭7) conveys a funky, down-home, bluesy sound and works over static minor chords lasting 2+ measures, often alternated with dorian minor for variety—though overuse should be avoided and jazz players typically extract licks rather than play it as scalar runs. Seventh chords layer a third atop triads, creating four diatonic types in minor (minor 7th, major 7th, dominant 7th, half-diminished 7th) with a fixed sequence when built on each degree of the dorian scale. The chapter's load-bearing terminology includes the blues scale formula, the four seventh-chord qualities, and positional voice names (root, third, fifth, seventh), with the core principle that all diatonic sevenths share tones with the parent minor scale and can function as both horizontal (melodic) and vertical (harmonic) material during improvisation over the tonic minor.

# Chapter 13 — Tension And Release; Related Scales And Modes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch13.md`._

Chapter 13 teaches the foundational principles of melodic tension and release in jazz improvisation, establishing that the root, third, and fifth are the consonant anchor tones while other scale tones create tension that must resolve either naturally (to consonant tones) or through expressive means like dynamic or tempo changes. The master's characteristic approach is systematic and architecturally comprehensive: seventh chords are practiced as scale-derived sets, phrases resolve on strong beats, and note placement relative to the pulse (ahead, on, or behind) creates distinct expressive characters—with beginners anchored to the beat before developing personal timing feel. The chapter introduces load-bearing terminology including the three-placement system (ahead/on/behind the beat), the dragging/rushing distinction, and the four-stage compositional sequence (statement, development, climax, release), while providing paired reference lists of tension-producing elements (ascending lines, wide intervals, dissonant harmony, repetition) and release elements (descending lines, chord tones, consonant harmony, silence) that codify how to shape solo architecture as a single dramatic arc building to climax then tapering off.

# Chapter 14 — Points To Keep In Mind & Articulations

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch14.md`._

This chapter establishes articulation as the second most critical element in jazz playing after note choice, and provides a systematic approach to developing it. The master's characteristic move is the reduction principle—just as 36 scales compress to 12 finger patterns, articulation mastery follows a progression from heavy to medium to light accents, practiced with metronome discipline across all keys rather than comfortable ones alone. The chapter introduces the core terminology of articulation types (heavy, medium, light, staccato, legato, breath, throat, stab, jab) and establishes that the foundational error is over-tonguing—the "tat tat tat" choppy feel that contradicts modern jazz's legato aesthetic. Key load-bearing concepts include the priority rule (articulation second only to note choice), the automaticity progression (exercises must become automatic before sounding natural), and the deliberate restriction of staccato to advanced players. The chapter's directive is unambiguous: develop on-demand accent control across your instrument's entire range and all keys, not just the easy ones.

# Chapter 15 — Nomenclature & Scale Syllabus

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch15.md`._

Chapter 15 introduces the foundational nomenclature and organizational principles for the Scale Syllabus that follows. The chapter establishes a reduced chord-symbol notation system (C, C7, C-, C∅, C-Δ, C7+9, C7b9) designed to minimize written clutter and free the improviser's mind from the page, along with a symbol legend defining half-steps, whole steps, and quality alterations. The master's characteristic pedagogical move is organizing all scale choices by dissonance level within each chord category, ranging from consonant sounds at the top to increasingly tense options below, grounded in the principle that music is made of tension and release—and the improviser's control of this balance determines communicative success. Load-bearing concepts include the chord/scale equivalence (each symbol represents a series of usable tones), the ear-voice-instrument learning method (sing the scale, play what you sang), and critical clarifications such as the complete tone content of abbreviated symbols like C7+9 (Diminished Whole Tone: Root, b9, +9, 3rd, +4, +5, b7) and C7b9 (Diminished: Root, b9, +9, 3rd, +4, 5th, 6th, b7), along with a practical preference order for minor scale choices favoring Dorian over Pure Minor.

# Chapter 16 — The Music Student's Plea & Tune Learning

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch16.md`._

The 3rd and 7th scale degrees form the harmonic foundation of any scale, determining its quality and harmonic motion, while dominant 7th chords resolve predictably up a perfect 4th with altered tones resolving by half-step. This chapter catalogs seven dominant 7th-based scales—DOM.7th, Bebop, Lydian Dominant, Whole-tone, Diminished, Dim. Whole-tone, and Spanish/Jewish—each with distinct characteristics: the Bebop scale places B natural only on upbeats, the Lydian Dominant features a raised 4th, the symmetrical Whole-tone and Diminished scales appear in cartoon music and are mastered by practitioners like Michael Brecker, and the Spanish/Jewish scale aligns with harmonic minor in minor keys. The chapter introduces key terminology around altered tones, symmetrical scales, and scale-degree functions, then prescribes experimentation with these scales over a cycle-of-fifths track to develop the proper scale usage that defines jazz appeal.

# Chapter 17 — Song Lists & Discography

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch17.md`._

This chapter presents a comprehensive 13-step procedure for internalizing jazz tunes, placing listening and ear training at the center of jazz musicianship. The master's characteristic moves—emphasizing the 3rd's and 7th's as guide tones, anchoring improvisation to the original melody, and mapping harmonic knowledge onto the instrument itself—reflect a philosophy that prioritizes ear development and melodic internalization over purely technical study. The chapter introduces no major new terminology but reinforces load-bearing concepts like guide tones, chord progression, and the distinction between melody-based and harmony-based improvisation, all organized around a single governing principle: that the ear, not notation or theory, is the best teacher, and that recordings contain all the answers needed for learning jazz.

# Chapter 18 — Scales, Patterns, and Chord Reference

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch18.md`._

Chapter 18 teaches systematic scale construction and practice methodology across whole tone, diminished, major, dominant, and minor scales, with emphasis on identifying chord tones (root, 3rd, 5th, 7th, 9th) within each scale up to the ninth. The chapter's characteristic moves include strict taxonomy—only three diminished scales exist, only two whole tone scales—and the prescription of ten canonical warm-up exercises to be practiced across all keys using six distinct root sequences for comprehensive coverage. Load-bearing terminology includes whole tone scale (WHWHWHWH pattern), diminished scale (WHWHWHWH with three instances), chord tone identification, and Dorian minor as a specific modal variant. The chapter emphasizes ear-first internalization through singing before instrumental transfer, positioning voice as the foundational tool for music learning.

# Chapter 19 — Practical Exercises

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch19.md`._

Chapter 19 teaches practical frameworks for mastering jazz fundamentals across all 12 keys, anchored by the Circle of Fourths (the harmonic foundation found everywhere in jazz) and practiced through transposition, a daily necessity for jazz musicians. The chapter emphasizes ear development via the singing-to-instrument method, contextual scale choice (blues scales for color but balanced with regular scales), and targeted harmonic awareness through 3rds and 7ths emphasis in dominant chords. Key technical rules include treating the 4th scale degree as a passing tone to avoid, maintaining even eighth-note phrasing in bossa nova contexts, and building phrases progressively from 2-bar units to longer 4, 6, or 8-bar forms (counting rests as structural elements). The master's characteristic moves visible here—the systematic 12-key memorization goal, the integration of ear training with instrumental practice, and the emphasis on harmonic awareness—reflect a pedagogical approach that treats transposition and harmonic literacy as inseparable from daily musicianship.

# Chapter 20 — Summary

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch20.md`._

Chapter 20 establishes memorization and transposition through all 12 keys as the foundational practice discipline, beginning with the II/V7 patterns and blues progression structured as three 4-bar phrases. The chapter articulates the master's characteristic moves: prioritizing what the inner ear hears over ego-driven technique, treating scales and chords as finite and learnable foundations for musical expression, and insisting on proper jazz articulation as a grassroots requirement in every phrase. The load-bearing terminology includes the conceptual model of blues as "three phrases" (beginning, middle, end), and the governing rule "play what your mind hears"—undergirded by lifelong ear training, memorization of melodies and progressions, and the permission to begin now rather than wait for perfection. The chapter culminates in reframing technical knowledge as a means to imagination, positioning sound support, articulation, and listening as non-negotiable practices that enable spontaneous expression.

      "name": "Twelve-Key Memorization Practice System",
      "summary": "This system governs how the student internalizes any scale, pattern, or progression across all 12 keys. The members are the ordered stages of the 12-step memorization procedure, organized as rungs of a progressive ladder. Traversal rules specify how the student advances through the rungs within a single key before moving to the next key, and how transposition proceeds. Modification rules cover ear-first internalization \u2014 singing before playing \u2014 and the adjustment of entry point based on the student's experience level.
